policy_statement: AF20 increases market access for New York low-carbon products by tracking markets, adopting low-carbon procurement, defining standards, incentivizing scale-up, financing assistance, and buyer/supplier connections.
mechanism_description: By supporting procurement and market connections for biochemicals and biomaterials made from agricultural and forest residues, the policy increases substitution for petrochemical-based products and reduces associated fossil-derived emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Procurement + market shaping → product substitution (biobased for petrochemical) → ↓ fossil feedstock use + ↓ petrochemical manufacturing emissions → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Public (and catalyzed private) purchasing, standards, and supplier-buyer linkages are altered to preferentially source certified low-carbon biochemicals/biomaterials made from agricultural/forest residues instead of conventional petrochemical-based products.  
(2) Mediating physical/industrial process: Material/feedstock substitution reduces demand for fossil-derived chemical feedstocks and associated energy-/process-intensive petrochemical refining and polymer/chemical manufacturing; depending on product pathways, some biogenic carbon may be stored temporarily in products, and avoided fossil combustion/process emissions dominate.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle CO₂e emissions (primarily CO₂ from fossil carbon and energy use; potentially also reduced associated upstream methane/leakage in oil & gas supply chains).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced lifecycle GHG emissions from substituting petrochemical products with lower-carbon biobased/biochemical alternatives enabled by procurement-driven market demand.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Procurement and market development drive substitution away from petrochemical products, yielding direct lifecycle GHG reductions—this is a mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it can improve material efficiency or circularity in some implementations, the defining, largest effect in this mechanism is lowering emissions via fossil-to-biobased product substitution, not “same service with less material/energy” (Resource Efficiency) nor hazard reduction (Adaptation) nor ecosystem restoration (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: If a policy’s main effect were waste reduction/reuse (resource efficiency) or forest/agriculture carbon sink enhancement (nature-based), classification could shift, but those are not required by this mechanism; the mechanism is explicitly procurement-enabled substitution to reduce fossil-derived product emissions.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that uses procurement/market access to increase adoption of residue-based biochemicals/biomaterials to replace petrochemical products is principally aiming at and achieving emissions reductions through avoided fossil-based production, so Mitigation remains primary across instances.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: The exact emissions sources (upstream oil & gas leakage vs. process energy vs. product carbon storage) and whether benefits are realized depends on product LCAs and implementation details; assigning secondary attributes should be done at the individual policy/evidence level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential edge cases include: (a) biobased products with high process energy or land-use impacts that erode/negate GHG benefits; (b) procurement focused on recycled-content or material reduction rather than bio-based substitution (would lean Resource Efficiency); (c) programs primarily aimed at rural economic development with unclear climate additionality. For the stated mechanism (residue-based biochemicals/biomaterials substituting petrochemicals), Mitigation is the most consistent primary category.
key_indicators: “low-carbon products”; “agricultural and forest residues are converted into biochemicals and biomaterials”; “replacements for the petrochemicals used to create packaging, cosmetics, binders, absorbents, concrete strengtheners”
co_benefits: Waste/residue valorization and reduced landfill/open burning; rural/forest-sector economic development; potential reductions in toxic co-pollutants associated with petrochemical production.
instance_edge_case_notes: The verbatim text alone does not specify an instrument (e.g., a procurement mandate/standard/incentive); classification relies on alignment with the stated AF20 procurement/market-access policy context rather than explicit instrument language in the excerpt.
